Q1: I tried to keep a steady routine, honestly. I usually studied in the evenings after my part-time job, maybe four or five times a week. I'd listen to recorded lectures while doing chores or commuting because that's how I absorb things best. But with limited resources at home, I couldn't always stick to my plan perfectly. I wrote out schedules in a notebook, but life kept interrupting them.
Q2: I really wanted to be consistent, and for the most part I managed to study at similar times each week. But when deadlines piled up or when I had extra shifts at work, everything got pushed around. Sometimes I'd have to cram everything into a weekend because the weekdays just slipped away from me. I tried my best to stay regular, but it wasn't always possible with everything else going on.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew there was something due soon or when the professor mentioned new materials in class. Sometimes I'd get anxious about missing something important, so I'd check just to make sure I wasn't falling behind. Honestly, fear of missing a deadline was probably my biggest motivator to log in. I didn't browse the LMS casually—it was always purpose-driven.
Q4: For a while in the middle of the semester, I got into a rhythm of checking the LMS every other day after dinner. It started because I was scared of missing announcements, and then it just became habit. But that routine broke down when things got stressful with exams and my job schedule changed. It never felt completely natural to me, more like something I forced myself to do.
Q5: The recorded lectures were everything for me since I learn best by listening. I'd play them multiple times, sometimes while walking to work or before bed. The discussion boards were also helpful because I could see how other students understood the material, which made me feel less alone. Anything audio-based or conversational worked well for how my brain processes information.
Q6: I mostly ignored the reading PDFs and supplementary articles because I just don't retain information well from reading on a screen. With my limited internet at home, downloading large files was a hassle anyway. I knew they were probably important, but I kept telling myself I'd get to them later and then never did. It wasn't that I didn't care—I just couldn't make them work for me.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could immediately see how it connected to what would be on the exam or in assignments. If it helped me understand a concept I was struggling with, that made it valuable. I also appreciated things that didn't require a lot of resources or stable internet to complete. When something felt disconnected from my grades, I struggled to justify spending time on it.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. With limited time and energy, I had to prioritize what was required just to pass. I'd look at optional materials and think 'that would be nice,' but then anxiety about the mandatory stuff would take over. Required activities carried this weight of fear—like if I didn't do them, everything would fall apart.
Q9: Yes, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download the audio lectures when I had good internet access and then listen to them repeatedly without being connected. I also borrowed textbooks from classmates and took handwritten notes that I'd review over and over. My phone had recordings of me reading my notes aloud, which I'd play back constantly. None of that showed up anywhere online.
Q10: There were whole weeks where I was studying intensely but barely touched the LMS. I'd be listening to downloaded lectures during my commute, quizzing myself from handwritten notes, or discussing concepts with a study partner in person. Sometimes I'd watch a video once online, then replay my downloaded version ten more times. The LMS only saw that single initial view.
Q11: Absolutely, that was my main strategy. I'd access a lecture or resource once, download it if possible, and then work with it offline for days. My internet at home isn't reliable, so I learned early on to grab what I needed when I could. I probably watched some lectures fifteen times total, but the system only recorded one or two views. It was survival mode, really.
Q12: Around midterms, everything felt impossible. I was scared—genuinely scared—that I wasn't going to make it through. Work was demanding more hours, I was exhausted, and the material was getting harder. My motivation just tanked, and even logging into the LMS felt like this huge mountain to climb. I'd stare at the login page and feel this wave of dread wash over me.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed by multiple deadlines, the LMS became something I avoided. Confusion about assignments made me anxious, and instead of seeking help, I'd sometimes just shut down. Competing responsibilities meant I had to make impossible choices about what got my attention. The fatigue was real—sometimes I'd log in and just not have the mental energy to actually do anything.
Q14: There were times I'd open videos or readings and just zone out completely. My eyes would be on the screen but my mind was somewhere else—worrying about bills, about work, about whether I was going to fail. I'd technically 'complete' an activity but retain nothing from it. Those moments felt like going through the motions without actually being present.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot, even though I knew participation mattered. Something about putting my thoughts out there for everyone to see terrified me. What if I said something wrong? What if people judged me? I'd tell myself I'd do it later when I felt more confident, but that confidence never really came. The fear of being wrong was paralyzing.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act sometimes. I'd see a due date approaching and suddenly find the energy I'd been missing. Most of my LMS activity probably clustered right before things were due. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was the push I needed to overcome my fear and avoidance. Without deadlines, I might have never engaged at all.
Q17: When I got feedback, it really affected me—sometimes too much. Positive comments made me want to try harder, but critical feedback sent me spiraling. I'd avoid checking grades for days because I was scared of what I'd see. Once I got a disappointing score, it took me a while to find the courage to engage with the course again. Feedback felt very high-stakes emotionally.
Q18: The weekly structure helped me know what to expect, which reduced some anxiety. But when modules built on each other and I fell behind, catching up felt overwhelming. I appreciated having a predictable rhythm, but the pace sometimes felt faster than I could manage with everything else in my life. A more flexible structure might have helped me breathe a little easier.
Q19: Having audio versions of content made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner. But the heavy reliance on reading materials and PDFs was really hard. Also, the discussion requirements stressed me out because I'm not confident in writing. If there had been more options—like voice recordings for participation—I think I would have engaged more comfortably.
Q20: I think sometimes people are trying really hard but just can't get the work done. They might be reading everything, watching every video, genuinely attempting to learn, but then hit a wall when it comes to producing something. Fear of failure, perfectionism, or just running out of time before deadlines—any of these could explain high activity with low submissions. It's not laziness; it's struggle.
Q21: That sounds a bit like me, actually. My LMS activity was pretty low, but I passed the course because so much of my learning happened offline. I listened to lectures repeatedly on my phone, studied from borrowed books, and talked through concepts with friends. The LMS didn't capture any of that effort. Low online activity doesn't mean someone isn't working hard.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone logged in and what they clicked on, but they can't show understanding or effort. They miss all the offline studying, the mental energy spent worrying about the course, the conversations with classmates. They also can't tell if someone was truly engaged during an activity or just going through the motions. Numbers don't capture the whole picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just getting through it. It's when you're mentally present, asking questions in your head, making connections to things you already know. For me, it also means feeling emotionally invested—wanting to succeed, being willing to push through fear. Engagement is as much about your internal state as your external actions.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was most of the time, or how hard I worked offline with limited resources. It wouldn't capture the hours spent listening to downloaded lectures on the bus, or the anxiety attacks before checking my grades. It also wouldn't show that I actually cared deeply about learning, even when my activity looked minimal. My engagement was real—it just wasn't always visible.
